p.a:
This work is licensed under a CC
Attribution 4.0 International License

presentation.title: The Hidden Common Ground in Business Agents and Agentic Coding
presentation.toc: Many organizations are treating business agents and agentic coding as separate trends, yet lack a clear understanding of whether they require fundamentally different platform investments. This fragmentation matters because it drives duplicated effort, inconsistent governance models, and misaligned API and AI strategies at a time when enterprises are under pressure to scale AI responsibly and efficiently. This talk looks at both use cases independently — examining their architectural, governance, and capability requirements — and then systematically compares them to reveal a shared platform substrate that is often overlooked. By clarifying their hidden common ground, this talk provides a more coherent roadmap for enterprise platform strategy, enabling organizations to align API, AI, and governance investments around a unified capability foundation rather than solving the same problem twice.

img.title: OpenAPI Initiative
img.style: float: right ; clear : both ; width : 30% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ;

ul.li: Head of Enterprise Strategy at Jentic
Ambassador for the OpenAPI Initiative (OAI)
They change how the business does things
Tool side speed-up
Getting existing work done faster
They change what the business does
Business-side reinvention
From narrow off-the-shelf to broad and ambitious
Different in obvious ways
Coding agents arrive via platform engineering
Business agents arrive via business strategy
Common in less obvious ways
97% of developers use coding agents
Business agents keep arriving, at least as COTS
Both need to plug in and integrate

img.title: Platform Engineering: Reduce repetitive tasks and make building things more effective
img.style: float: right ; height : 50% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ; filter: drop-shadow(0px 0px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) ;

ul.li: You don't need a platform to ship one thing
Platforms are for shipping many similar things at scale
Shared substrate beats many bespoke stacks
Capabilities, governance, security → built for reuse
That's where both kinds of agents are heading
Coding agents: already at scale; platform spirit emerging
Business agents: scale is the next problem to solve

img.title: Stephen Fishman and Matt McLarty, "Unbundling the Enterprise: APIs, Optionality, and the Science of Happy Accidents", 2024
img.style: float: right ; height : 70% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ; filter: drop-shadow(0px 0px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) ;

ul.li: Fitness = the ability to experiment and learn fast
AI is where that pays off most right now
Disconnected stacks → fragmented optionality
Shared platform → unified optionality
Many reports on AI scaling but none on this disconnect
We surveyed both sides
IT / Platform engineering
Business
200+ responses across 50 organizations
Found 7 disconnects
Today: 2 of them
Agents getting connected to APIs (directly or via MCP)
API quality and AI-readiness not checked first
Broken or sub-optimal workflows now exposed to agents
Old problems carry into the new AI layer
API problems may surface as AI problems

img.title: What happens when you hand your agents bad APIs

ul.li: Platform foundations skipped under C-suite AI pressure
AI pilots: isolated results, scaling not part of the plan
Bad processes get scaled up, not fixed

slide.title: Tactics: MCP-Washing is not a Strategy

img.style: float: right ; height : 70% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ; filter: drop-shadow(0px 0px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) ;

ul.li: Make AI-readiness a gate before agent integrations
Reuse and refactor existing API governance
Fix bad APIs & workflows before agents touch them
Don't paper over old problems with MCP
Agents are less predictable than traditional code
Coding agents produce code: reviewable before it runs
Business agents produce actions: live as they're decided
Both need limits but controls in place are mostly symbolic
Some controls exist, but no one is watching
Vulnerabilities and unusual patterns invisible
Context and action creep: agents access more than they need
Optionality takes a hit when experiments feel unsafe
Add a control layer between agents and APIs
Default-deny: grant only what's needed
Monitor what agents do, not just that they have keys
Alerts + thresholds → human-in-the-loop

img.title: Immanuel Kant: "An ideal is a regulative idea"

ul.li: One agent platform is the ideal, not the overnight plan
Treat both kinds of agents as one demand stream
Share governance, security, observability
Spot parallel platforms and manage them strategically

img.title: This is the End
img.style: float: right ; height : 65% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ; filter: drop-shadow(0px 0px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) ;

img.title: OpenAPI Initiative
img.style: float: right ; clear : both ; width : 30% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ;

ul.li: Head of Enterprise Strategy at Jentic
Ambassador for the OpenAPI Initiative (OAI)

slide.title: A Brief History of OAI & OpenAPI

ul.li: Started out in 2011 as Swagger
Swagger was renamed to OpenAPI at the beginning of 2016
Donated to the OpenAPI Initiative (OAI) (part of the Linux Foundation)
Swagger now refers to tools offered by SmartBear
OpenAPI has been the official name since 2016
OpenAPI 2.0 was the name after the rename (it's the same as Swagger 2.0)
OpenAPI 3.0 was released in July 2017
OpenAPI 3.1 was released in February 2021
OpenAPI 3.2 was released in September 2025

img.title: This is the End
img.style: float: right ; height : 65% ; margin : 0% 4% 4% 4% ; filter: drop-shadow(0px 0px 10px rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) ;
